summary: Crystal‑clear SBD second set with a monster Shakedown Street jam makes this fall ’79 show shine despite an audience‑sourced first half.
review: The recording delivers superb fidelity – the first set is an audience capture with some ambient noise, while the second set switches to a pristine soundboard source that reviewers call “phatty” and “crisp.” Musically the night is a highlight of the ’79 tour: a funk‑filled Shakedown Street opener, incendiary Passenger, razor‑tight Estimated Prophet, an otherworldly Eyes of the World, and a soaring Friend of the Devil, all punctuated by stellar keyboards from Brent Mydland and fiery guitar from Jerry Garcia.

ai_rating.rationale: Over 30 reviews overwhelmingly praise both the performance (standout second‑set jams) and the audio quality (crisp SBD source), yielding a rating in the excellent range.

recording_quality.technical_notes: First set sourced from audience mic – noticeable ambient chatter but still clear. Second set is a high‑fidelity soundboard (SBD) that reviewers describe as crisp, phatty, with superb channel separation and minimal noise.

show_quality.setlist_flow: The first set is solid but less memorable; the second set erupts into a jam‑heavy monster featuring a funky Shakedown Street opener, seamless transitions into Space/Drums, and extended improvisations that keep the energy soaring throughout.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Repeatedly praised for fiery, on‑fire guitar work – “floating raging” on Tiger Rose and leading the Shakedown Street jam.
band_member_comments.Phil: Described as blistering and space‑filled; his bass electronics are called “organ‑shaking” and a one‑of‑a‑kind jam driver.
band_member_comments.Bob: Noted for rocking out during the set and delivering strong vocals on Good Lovin'.
band_member_comments.Keys: Brent Mydland highlighted for clavinet, Rhodes, and synth bombs that add funk to Shakedown Street; keyboards praised throughout the second half.
band_member_comments.Drums: Channel separation lauded; the drums/space transition is called “awesome weirdness” with tight execution.
